questions.scenario: The orders API is deployed to Container Apps. Users report intermittent failures after 10 percent of traffic moved to a new revision. What should the team inspect first?

options.text: The active revisions, traffic weights, image tags, health state, and logs for the candidate revision.
The billing account, because traffic movement usually changes cost.
The VM agent status, because all Azure compute depends on VM agents.
The public DNS zone only, because users are reaching the service through a name.

questions.explanation: The symptom changed when revision traffic changed. The first evidence should come from the Container Apps release surface.

questions.scenario: A web app starts, but the health endpoint fails only in production. Which explanation should be checked before replacing the hosting service?

options.text: The app service plan is always too small when health fails.
The runtime configuration, secret references, managed identity, and dependency endpoints may differ from staging.
Azure App Service cannot run production APIs reliably.
The deployment slot must be deleted and recreated.

questions.explanation: Health can fail because the production runtime starts with different inputs and permissions. Changing the hosting service before checking that evidence adds noise.

questions.scenario: Which signals help decide whether a compute incident belongs to the runtime layer rather than the database schema or application code alone?

options.text: The deployed image or runtime version differs from the expected release.
The service cannot read a secret or connect with its managed identity.
The health endpoint fails before meaningful application traffic reaches business logic.
The resource group name contains the word prod.

questions.explanation: Runtime evidence shows how Azure started and routed the process. Naming is helpful context, but it is not runtime proof.
